intro: Transitioning from C++ to Scala shifts development from an imperative, hardware-aware architecture to a highly expressive hybrid functional-object ecosystem running on the JVM or Scala Native. Scala trades C++ manual pointer operations and templates for strong functional primitives, type-safe macros, and advanced pattern matching. This eliminates memory corruption classes while heavily scaling concurrent data architectures.

diffs.from: Static and nominal with unsafe casts, template specialization, structural overloading, and platform-dependent types.
diffs.to: Static, advanced type system featuring path-dependent types, structural types, implicits/givens, traits, and generalized type constraints.

diffs.from: Low-level imperative style requiring explicit types, macro preprocessors, semicolons, and manual memory access operators.
diffs.to: High-level, expressive syntax combining object-oriented and functional designs, favoring expressions over statements and omitting semicolons.

diffs.from: Deterministic allocation managed via stack scopes, explicit heap requests, and automated RAII smart pointer hierarchies.
diffs.to: Automatic memory abstraction managed by a garbage collector, prioritizing immutable object references and persistent data structures.

diffs.from: Low-level threading via std::thread, synchronization primitives, or system-level non-blocking event loops.
diffs.to: Functional concurrency using Monadic Futures, Monad Transformers, or advanced effect systems like Cats Effect and ZIO.

diffs.from: Standard Template Library (STL) focused on algorithmic primitives, execution policies, and bare-metal abstractions.
diffs.to: Deeply integrated functional collection suite featuring immutable structures, advanced stream APIs, and pattern-matching toolsets.

examples.title: Pattern Matching vs Switch-Case
examples.description: Demonstrates switching over types and structures using C++ variant matching vs Scala's powerful native pattern matching.
examples.toCode:
sealed trait Msg
case class IntMsg(value: Int) extends Msg
case class StrMsg(value: String) extends Msg

def process(m: Msg): Unit = m match {
  case IntMsg(v) => println(s"Int: $v")
  case StrMsg(v) => println(s"Str: $v")
}

examples.title: Immutable Collection Mapping
examples.description: Transforms a vector using an inline lambda mutation in C++ versus a clean, functional map expression in Scala.
examples.toCode:
def square(source: List[Int]): List[Int] = 
  source.map(x => x * x)

tips.title: Embrace Immutability By Default
tips.body: Replace C++ mutable states and pointer references with Scala 'val' assignments and immutable collections. This guarantees thread safety and completely eliminates side-effect bugs.

tips.title: Convert Templates to Traits and Givens
tips.body: Translate complex C++ compile-time template metaprogramming structures into Scala Traits for polymorphic behavior and 'given/using' instances (Scala 3) for contextual abstractions.

tips.title: Rethink Errors as Values
tips.body: Instead of throwing runtime exceptions or returning error codes, use Scala's 'Option', 'Either', or 'Try' types to force explicit compile-time error handling within the type signature.

tips.title: Leverage Case Classes for Modeling
tips.body: Avoid boilerplate object modeling. Use Scala 'case class' constructs to instantaneously gain structural equality, unapply extractors for pattern matching, and built-in serialization mechanisms.
